finding: Rapid category-based associations automatically activate and influence behavior in proportion to associative strength, but perceivers can intentionally reverse or suppress this effect when cognitive resources permit; automatic effects persist under extreme resource constraint but remain modifiable by intention.

Established nonprejudiced credentials increase willingness to express ambiguous attitudes interpretable as prejudiced by reframing future behavior as consistent with established character; effect depends on self-image reassurance rather than external impression management.

Effortful self-regulation in one domain temporarily depletes capacity for regulation in subsequent unrelated domains; depletion follows strength model rather than priming or skill models, suggesting single underlying regulatory resource exhausted by exertion.

Adult development yields three distinct positive mental health patterns (Achievers, Conservers, Seekers) with different life goals and strengths; each pattern is predicted by distinctive personality-motivation profiles at age 21 mediated through identity consolidation at age 43.

People believe they know their peers' dispositions better than their peers know theirs, attributing this asymmetry to differential diagnosticity of observable behavior (revealing others) vs. private thoughts (revealing self); effect persists despite evidence of equal accuracy and reflects fundamental attribution error applied asymmetrically.
